danger_signals.meaning: Platformer convention: enemies appear as obstacles to avoid or defeat
Typical platformer death zones (pits, obstacles) inferred from genre conventions

safety_signals.meaning: Inferred platformer convention: pickups restore health or grant power-ups
Standard platformer mechanic: solid ground for navigation

faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Generic enemies typical to 1989 NES platformers

world_state_to_visual.world_state: Time period setting (1955, 1985, or 1985 alternate timeline inferred from film)
world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Likely reflected in level design and background art changes across stages
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Different enemies or obstacles per time period

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Music tempo (documented as 300 BPM, making 'Power of Love' and 'Johnny B. Goode' unrecognizable, potentially conveying tension)
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Fast-paced gameplay typical to NES platformers

environmental_storytelling.example: 'Power of Love' by Huey Lewis & the News and 'Johnny B. Goode' by Chuck Berry (both from the 1985 film) establish the Back to the Future setting through audio cues
Multiple gameplay modes suggest different scenarios or time periods (inferred from 'game with multiple modes of play' description)

entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: Player-controlled platforming character navigating obstacles and enemies
Standard NES platformer AI: likely patrol routes with collision-based attack responses

notes: Sources provide minimal direct information on visual communication systems. Data extracted is primarily inferred from: (1) confirmed game paradigm (platformer), (2) confirmed media (NES 1989), (3) licensed music as narrative device, and (4) standard genre conventions. The game's 'multiple modes of play' suggests state-based level variation, potentially across time periods from the film, but specific visual manifestations are not documented in available sources. The notorious 300 BPM music tempo issue suggests the game may communicate urgency/danger through audio intensity, though this appears to be an implementation error rather than intentional design.
